posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: About a year and a half ago, I was talking with Neal Rosenthal’s west coast regional manager, Ben Andersen, about Levet. At the time, Rosenthal had in his inventory 05, 06, 07, 08, 09 and 10 Journaries and Chavaroche. I had just drunk the 10 Chavaroche and explained to Ben that I was tempted to buy every case that Rosenthal had remaining (about 50, as I recall), tuck it into a cold, dark corner of my underground storage for about 20 years and then look like a genius. Ben explained that Bernard Levet considers his 2010s to be the best wines he has made since the 1978s, and told me to “go for it.” Sadly, the following week, I decided to purchase some but not all of Rosenthal’s remaining 2010 Chavaroche and later Rosenthal himself decided to hold back what he had remaining for a “library” release in the future. Ugh! Thus, I cut a large swath through Rosenthal’s then-remaining 2010 Journaries inventory. Earlier this week, I noticed a bottle of it in my warehouse with a wrinkled label (Levet’s bottling machine, probably a person, applies a few of the labels in a higgledy piggledy fashion, ala Rayas), so I grabbed it for the sake of science. Levet’s Journaries cuvee comes entirely from old-vine La Landonne fruit and, while it may not be as “ferocious” (as Rosenthal says) as Chavaroche, it is positively in the same mold. The 2010 Journaries has a beguiling aroma of flowers, blue/black fruit and game. It hits the palate with intensity and is taut, muscled and firm, jam-packed with bare-naked, iron-fed northern Rhone syrah the way Mother Nature, not man, makes it. It’s a singular wine that will make for a wild, full-throttle drinking experience for a long time. Yes, it’s big, but beautifully proportioned.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked: Good not to be greedy.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Thanks for a useful note. I have both wines, and will now consider a bottle of the Journaries in the near future.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
Markus S:

Good not to be greedy.

In all events, they would have been shared.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
Markus S:

Good not to be greedy.

Tell Rosenthal who will do the “library” release at a much inflated price.
The 05 Journaries was in a beautiful place when I had it a few weeks ago. Not in a note-taking environment, but it was awesome stuff.

D@vid Bu3ker:
Markus S:

Good not to be greedy.

Tell Rosenthal who will do the “library” release at a much inflated price.

No doubt about that!

Not necessarily. Rosenthal held back the 2005s and released them at very fair prices - about $5-$10 more than the current release.

Martin Steinley:

Not necessarily. Rosenthal held back the 2005s and released them at very fair prices - about $5-$10 more than the current release.

True. And the 05 was priced more reasonably than many current release Cote Roties.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked: sounds like a giant in the making.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: This is a great wine, but don’t miss out on the extremely elegant 2011 in the rush to acquire the 2010!

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

This is a great wine, but don’t miss out on the extremely elegant 2011 in the rush to acquire the 2010!

Agreed, William. I believe that you can throw a dart at the 04-12 Levet wines and do just fine.
I don’t know anything other than the Chavaroche…but I’m glad to be having a few in the cellar to age.

posts.name: todd waldmann
posts.cooked: Sounds delightful Martin. Your commitment to the dirty work of opening these bottles in the name of science is commendable.

posts.name: Ross Massey
posts.cooked:
Martin Steinley:

Agreed, William. I believe that you can throw a dart at the 04-12 Levet wines and do just fine.

I’d agree except for 2008-- I’ve mixed luck with that year. Aside from that it’s just a matter of where you want to be on the ripeness spectrum (from 09/07 at the big end down to 04/06/11).
